nodejs.title: What is `npm` and what are its core functionalities?
nodejs.description: This question assesses the candidate's basic understanding of Node.js's package manager and its primary uses.
nodejs.answer: npm stands for Node Package Manager. It is the default package manager for Node.js and is used for installing, sharing, and managing project dependencies. Its core functionalities include installing packages from the npm registry, managing project metadata in `package.json`, running scripts defined in `package.json`, and publishing your own packages to the registry. It's crucial for building and maintaining Node.js applications efficiently.

nodejs.title: How do you handle environment variables in Node.js?
nodejs.description: This question checks how candidates manage configuration for different deployment environments.
nodejs.answer: Environment variables are a standard way to externalize configuration from your code, making applications more flexible and secure. In Node.js, they are accessed via `process.env`. For local development, a common practice is to use a `.env` file with a library like `dotenv` to load these variables into `process.env`. In production, they are usually set directly in the deployment environment (e.g., Heroku config vars, Kubernetes secrets).

nodejs.title: What are Worker Threads in Node.js and when would you use them?
nodejs.description: This question explores understanding of Node.js's approach to CPU-bound tasks and parallelism.
nodejs.answer: Worker Threads are a feature in Node.js that allows for running CPU-intensive JavaScript operations in parallel, offloading them from the main event loop. This prevents the main thread from blocking, ensuring the application remains responsive. You would use them for tasks like complex calculations, data compression, or heavy cryptographic operations that are CPU-bound rather than I/O-bound. They communicate with the main thread via message passing.

nodejs.title: Describe the `Buffer` class in Node.js and its use cases.
nodejs.description: This question assesses knowledge of how Node.js handles binary data, which is essential for low-level operations.
nodejs.answer: The `Buffer` class in Node.js is a global object used to handle raw binary data. It represents a fixed-size chunk of memory (outside the V8 heap) that cannot be resized. Buffers are commonly used when interacting with streams, file systems, network protocols, or any operation involving binary data directly, such as reading images, audio, or encoding/decoding data. They are crucial for efficiency when dealing with non-UTF8 encoded data.

nodejs.title: How do you secure your Node.js API endpoints?
nodejs.description: This question checks understanding of common security practices for web APIs built with Node.js.
nodejs.answer: Securing Node.js API endpoints involves several strategies. This typically includes implementing authentication (e.g., JWT, OAuth) and authorization to control access, using HTTPS to encrypt communication, validating and sanitizing all user input to prevent injection attacks (XSS, SQLi), rate limiting to prevent brute-force attacks, and setting appropriate HTTP security headers (e.g., Helmet.js). Regularly updating dependencies is also critical.

nodejs.title: Explain the concept of 'event-driven architecture' in Node.js.
nodejs.description: This question explores a core architectural paradigm that Node.js heavily relies on.
nodejs.answer: Node.js embraces an event-driven architecture, where an application listens for and reacts to events. Instead of traditional synchronous request-response models, operations are initiated, and when they complete (an 'event'), a predefined 'listener' function is triggered. This non-blocking nature, powered by the Event Loop, allows Node.js to handle many concurrent connections efficiently without creating a new thread for each, making it highly scalable for I/O-bound applications.

nodejs.title: What is the purpose of `package-lock.json`?
nodejs.description: This question tests knowledge of dependency management specifics, crucial for reproducible builds.
nodejs.answer: `package-lock.json` is automatically generated by npm (or yarn.lock by Yarn) and records the exact version tree of `node_modules` at the time it was generated. Its primary purpose is to ensure that subsequent installations of the project by anyone will result in the exact same `node_modules` directory structure, regardless of minor version updates in package dependencies. This guarantees consistent and reproducible builds across different environments and team members.

nodejs.title: How do you manage sessions and authentication in a Node.js web application?
nodejs.description: This question assesses practical knowledge of common web development concerns with Node.js.
nodejs.answer: Session management and authentication in Node.js typically involve storing session data server-side (e.g., in a database like Redis) and sending a session ID (often via a cookie) to the client. Libraries like `express-session` simplify this. For stateless authentication, JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) are common, where a token is issued upon successful login and sent with each subsequent request, verified on the server without storing session data on the server itself. Middleware is used to protect routes.

nodejs.title: When would you use `child_process` module in Node.js?
nodejs.description: This question delves into understanding how Node.js can interact with the operating system and execute external programs.
nodejs.answer: The `child_process` module allows Node.js to spawn new processes that can run arbitrary shell commands or external programs. You would use it when you need to execute system commands, interact with legacy applications, or run CPU-bound tasks in a separate process that Node.js itself isn't well-suited for (e.g., complex image processing, invoking a Python script). It offers methods like `exec`, `spawn`, `fork`, and `execFile` for different use cases and interaction patterns.

nodejs.title: What are some common ways to improve performance in a Node.js application?
nodejs.description: This question evaluates understanding of optimization techniques specific to Node.js applications.
nodejs.answer: Improving Node.js performance involves several strategies: optimizing database queries and using caching (e.g., Redis), leveraging Worker Threads for CPU-bound tasks, using efficient asynchronous patterns (async/await), implementing proper error handling, utilizing HTTP/2, using a reverse proxy (like Nginx), and load balancing across multiple Node.js instances. Profiling and monitoring the application to identify bottlenecks is also crucial. Keeping the event loop unblocked is paramount.

nodejs.title: Describe the Node.js module system. What are CommonJS and ES Modules?
nodejs.description: This question covers a foundational aspect of Node.js application structure and dependency management.
nodejs.answer: Node.js uses a module system to organize code into reusable units. Historically, it primarily used CommonJS, where modules are loaded synchronously using `require()` and expose functionality via `module.exports` or `exports`. With newer Node.js versions, ES Modules (ESM) are also supported, using `import` and `export` keywords. ESM offers features like static analysis and tree-shaking, and is the standard for modern JavaScript. Node.js can run both, but ESM requires specific file extensions (`.mjs`) or `type: 'module'` in `package.json`.

nodejs.title: How do you handle graceful shutdown in a Node.js application?
nodejs.description: This question assesses knowledge of how to properly terminate a Node.js application without data loss or service disruption.
nodejs.answer: Graceful shutdown ensures that a Node.js application can terminate cleanly without losing data or active requests. This typically involves listening for termination signals (like `SIGTERM` or `SIGINT`). Upon receiving such a signal, the application should stop accepting new connections, finish processing existing requests, close database connections, release other resources, and then exit. Libraries like `http-shutdown` or custom logic can facilitate this process.

nodejs.title: What is the purpose of `__dirname` and `__filename` in Node.js?
nodejs.description: This question checks understanding of common global variables used for path resolution in Node.js.
nodejs.answer: `__dirname` is a global variable that holds the absolute path to the directory containing the currently executing script. `__filename` is a global variable that holds the absolute path to the currently executing file, including its filename. They are useful for constructing file paths that are relative to the script's location, ensuring portability across different environments. With ES Modules, these are not directly available and often require `import.meta.url` combined with `path` and `url` modules.

nodejs.title: Explain how clustering works in Node.js.
nodejs.description: This question assesses understanding of how Node.js leverages multi-core CPUs despite its single-threaded nature.
nodejs.answer: Node.js `cluster` module allows you to create child processes (workers) that share the same server port. This enables a single Node.js application to take advantage of multi-core systems by distributing incoming requests across multiple worker processes. Each worker runs on its own event loop and can handle requests independently, effectively scaling the application horizontally on a single machine. The master process manages the workers, restarting them if they crash.

nodejs.title: What is the role of the V8 JavaScript engine in Node.js?
nodejs.description: This question delves into the underlying technology powering Node.js execution.
nodejs.answer: The V8 JavaScript engine, developed by Google, is the core component that executes JavaScript code in Node.js. It compiles JavaScript directly to machine code, providing high performance. V8 also handles memory management (garbage collection) and call stack management. Node.js bundles V8, along with `libuv` (for I/O operations) and other libraries, to create its runtime environment. This allows Node.js to execute server-side JavaScript efficiently.

nodejs.title: How do you handle CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) in Node.js?
nodejs.description: This question checks practical knowledge of a common web security feature and its implementation.
nodejs.answer: CORS is a security mechanism that allows or restricts web applications running at one domain to make requests to a resource at another domain. In Node.js, particularly with Express.js, CORS is commonly handled by setting appropriate HTTP headers on the server. The `cors` npm package is a popular middleware that simplifies this. It allows you to configure which origins, methods, and headers are permitted, enabling controlled cross-origin requests while maintaining security.

nodejs.title: What are some best practices for structuring a Node.js project?
nodejs.description: This question explores understanding of project organization and maintainability.
nodejs.answer: Good project structure promotes maintainability and scalability. Common best practices include: organizing code by feature or module, separating concerns (e.g., controllers, services, models), using an `src` directory for source code, defining clear API routes, and using environment variables for configuration. Additionally, having separate folders for tests, using a linter (ESLint) and formatter (Prettier), and clear documentation are beneficial. Avoid monolithic files and use a consistent naming convention.

nodejs.title: Explain 'dependency injection' and how it can be used in Node.js.
nodejs.description: This question assesses knowledge of a fundamental software design pattern for modularity and testability.
nodejs.answer: Dependency Injection (DI) is a design pattern where components receive their dependencies from an external source rather than creating them internally. In Node.js, this means passing dependencies (like database connections, service modules, or configuration objects) as arguments to functions or constructors. This promotes loose coupling, making code more modular, testable (through mocking dependencies), and easier to maintain. While there are DI containers, it's often done manually with plain JavaScript.

nodejs.title: How would you implement caching in a Node.js application?
nodejs.description: This question probes into strategies for improving application responsiveness and reducing database load.
nodejs.answer: Caching in Node.js can be implemented at various levels. For in-memory caching, libraries like `node-cache` or simple JavaScript objects can store frequently accessed data. For distributed caching across multiple instances, external stores like Redis or Memcached are ideal. You would typically cache data that changes infrequently, or the results of expensive computations/database queries. Middleware can be used to check the cache before hitting the database, significantly improving response times and reducing server load.

nodejs.title: What is the difference between readFile and createReadStream in Node.js?
nodejs.description: Explain how these two file reading approaches handle system memory and data processing differently.
nodejs.answer: `fs.readFile` reads the entire file into memory buffer asynchronously before invoking its callback, making it unsuitable for very large files. On the other hand, `fs.createReadStream` reads the file in small, manageable chunks (default 64KB) using streams, allowing you to process data sequentially as it arrives. Using streams prevents high memory consumption and avoids potential application crashes when dealing with large datasets.

nodejs.title: How does the thread pool work in Node.js?
nodejs.description: Describe how Node.js executes blocking operations that cannot be handled by the single-threaded event loop.
nodejs.answer: While JavaScript execution is single-threaded, Node.js offloads heavy, blocking I/O operations (like cryptographic, compression, and file system tasks) to a thread pool managed by `libuv`. By default, this thread pool size is four, but it can be adjusted using the `UV_THREADPOOL_SIZE` environment variable. Once a worker thread completes its assigned task, it signals the main thread to run the associated callback in the event loop.

nodejs.title: Explain the difference between require() and import in Node.js.
nodejs.description: Contrast the CommonJS module system with the modern ES modules system in Node.js runtime environments.
nodejs.answer: `require()` is the synchronous, runtime loading mechanism used by the CommonJS module system, while `import` is the static, compile-time loading mechanism used by ECMAScript Modules (ESM). Because `require()` is dynamic, it can be called conditionally inside `if` statements, whereas standard `import` statements must reside at the top-level of the file. To use `import` in Node.js, you must either use the `.mjs` extension or set `"type": "module"` in `package.json`.

nodejs.title: What is the purpose of the cluster module and how does it utilize multi-core CPUs?
nodejs.description: Explain how to scale a Node.js application vertically to take full advantage of multi-core servers.
nodejs.answer: The `cluster` module allows you to spawn a network of child processes (workers) that all share the exact same server ports. The master process uses a round-robin load balancing strategy by default to distribute incoming HTTP requests among the active worker instances. This allows single-threaded Node.js applications to utilize all available CPU cores of the host machine, significantly increasing overall throughput.

nodejs.title: How do you handle uncaught exceptions and unhandled promise rejections in Node.js?
nodejs.description: Detail the global event listeners used to catch unexpected application-level errors and prevent silent failures.
nodejs.answer: You can listen to global process events using `process.on('uncaughtException', callback)` and `process.on('unhandledRejection', callback)`. When an uncaught exception occurs, the application is in an undefined state, so it is best practice to log the error and perform a graceful shutdown using `process.exit(1)`. For unhandled promise rejections, Node.js will automatically terminate the process in modern versions unless handled explicitly.

nodejs.title: What is backpressure in Node.js streams and how do you handle it?
nodejs.description: Explain the scenario where data production outpaces data consumption and how to resolve it.
nodejs.answer: Backpressure occurs when a readable stream produces data faster than the writable stream can consume it, causing data to accumulate in the memory buffer. To handle this, writable streams return `false` from `.write()` when their internal buffer limits are exceeded, signaling the readable stream to pause. Using `.pipe()` automatically manages this coordination underneath, pausing and resuming the stream flow to prevent memory exhaustion.

nodejs.title: What are memory leaks in Node.js and how do you detect them?
nodejs.description: Explain common sources of memory leaks in long-running Node.js processes and how to troubleshoot them.
nodejs.answer: Memory leaks in Node.js happen when garbage collection cannot reclaim unused memory, often caused by global variables, active event listeners that aren't removed, or forgotten closures. You can detect them by running Node.js with the `--inspect` flag and capturing heap snapshots over time using Chrome DevTools. Analyzing these snapshots helps identify which objects are continuously growing in size without being freed.

nodejs.title: Explain how the EventEmitter class works under the hood.
nodejs.description: Describe the implementation structure and logic of the Observer pattern implemented by EventEmitter.
nodejs.answer: Under the hood, an `EventEmitter` maintains an internal dictionary object where keys are event names and values are arrays of registered callback functions. When `.on('eventName', callback)` is called, the function is pushed into the array for that event key. When `.emit('eventName', data)` is invoked, the emitter iterates synchronously through the array of callbacks and executes each one with the provided arguments.

nodejs.title: What is the purpose of the util.promisify function in Node.js?
nodejs.description: Explain how to convert older callback-style APIs into modern promise-based APIs.
nodejs.answer: `util.promisify` is a utility function that takes a standard Node.js callback-style function (where the callback is the last argument and expects `(err, value)`) and returns a new function that returns a Promise. This allows developers to easily modernize older legacy APIs to work seamlessly with `async/await` syntax. For example, `const fsPromises = util.promisify(fs.readFile)` allows you to await file reads directly.

nodejs.title: What is the role of the path module and why is it preferred over manual string concatenation?
nodejs.description: Explain how the path module resolves cross-platform file pathing inconsistencies.
nodejs.answer: The `path` module provides utilities for working with file and directory paths across different operating systems, which use different separators (e.g., `/` on Unix vs `\` on Windows). Using `path.join()` or `path.resolve()` ensures that your application remains platform-independent by automatically choosing the correct separator. It also cleans up redundant slashes and relative path segments like `.` and `..` automatically.

nodejs.title: Explain the differences between fork(), spawn(), and exec() in the child_process module.
nodejs.description: Contrast the three primary ways to spawn child processes in Node.js.
nodejs.answer: `spawn` launches a process asynchronously and streams its output in real-time, making it ideal for large amounts of data. `exec` spawns a shell and buffers the entire output in memory before passing it to a callback, which is best for running short CLI commands. `fork` is a specialized version of `spawn` designed specifically for running Node.js modules, establishing an IPC communication channel between the parent and child.

nodejs.title: How do you protect a Node.js API against Denial of Service (DoS) and brute-force attacks?
nodejs.description: Discuss architectural and middleware strategies for rate-limiting incoming API requests.
nodejs.answer: To prevent DoS and brute-force attacks, you should implement rate-limiting middleware such as `express-rate-limit` or use a distributed token-bucket strategy with Redis. Additionally, you should limit request body sizes using body-parsing middleware settings to prevent clients from sending massive payloads. On a system level, deploying a reverse proxy like NGINX or Cloudflare in front of your Node.js application provides robust rate-limiting and DDoS mitigation.

nodejs.title: What is the difference between dependencies, devDependencies, and peerDependencies?
nodejs.description: Distinguish between the different dependency classifications in a package.json file.
nodejs.answer: `dependencies` are required for the application to run in production (e.g., Express). `devDependencies` are only needed during local development and testing (e.g., Jest, ESLint). `peerDependencies` specify that your package requires a specific version of a host tool or library to be installed by the consuming project itself, which is common when writing plugins for libraries like React.

nodejs.title: How does Node.js handle crypto operations efficiently without blocking the event loop?
nodejs.description: Explain how cryptographic operations utilize lower-level resources for high performance.
nodejs.answer: Cryptographic operations like hashing or key derivation are highly CPU-intensive and would block a single-threaded event loop if executed synchronously. To prevent this, Node.js's native `crypto` module offers asynchronous alternatives (like `crypto.pbkdf2`) that offload the work to the libuv thread pool. These asynchronous functions run concurrently in C++ background threads, returning the final result via callback or promise without halting main thread execution.

nodejs.title: Explain the concept of 'Event Loop Pollution' and how to avoid it.
nodejs.description: Detail the negative impacts of blocking the event loop with synchronous computation.
nodejs.answer: Event Loop Pollution (or event loop blocking) occurs when synchronous, CPU-bound code runs on the main thread for a prolonged period, preventing other microtasks, macrotasks, and I/O callbacks from executing. This leads to high response latency and application-wide freezing. You can avoid it by breaking up heavy tasks with `setImmediate()`, offloading computations to Worker Threads, or delegation to external queue-based worker processes.

nodejs.title: How can you securely store and verify passwords in a Node.js application?
nodejs.description: Outline the standard cryptographic flow for user password management.
nodejs.answer: You should never store passwords in plain text; instead, use a strong hashing algorithm like `bcrypt` or `argon2` that includes built-in salting. Salting ensures that identical passwords result in different hashes, protecting against rainbow table attacks. When a user logs in, use the library's verification function (e.g., `bcrypt.compare`) which performs a constant-time comparison to protect against timing attacks.

nodejs.title: What are stream piping and the .pipe() method in Node.js?
nodejs.description: Explain the concept of chaining readable and writable streams.
nodejs.answer: Piping is a mechanism that connects the output of a Readable stream directly to the input of a Writable stream using the `.pipe()` method. This allows data to flow seamlessly from source to destination without loading the entire dataset into application memory. It also automatically handles backpressure management, pausing and resuming the readable source as the destination buffer fills and empties.

nodejs.title: What is the purpose of the AbortController in modern Node.js?
nodejs.description: Explain how to cancel ongoing asynchronous tasks such as fetch requests or event listeners.
nodejs.answer: `AbortController` is a web-standard API integrated into Node.js that allows you to cancel asynchronous operations like HTTP fetch requests, file reads, or timeouts on demand. It provides an `AbortSignal` object which you pass to the async operation. When you call `controller.abort()`, the signal triggers an abort event, causing the associated promise to reject with an `AbortError` and immediately free up system resources.

nodejs.title: How do you handle database connection pooling in a Node.js application?
nodejs.description: Explain how connection pooling optimizes communication with databases under heavy loads.
nodejs.answer: Instead of opening and closing a new database connection for every single incoming request—which is resource-intensive and slow—you use connection pooling. A pool maintains a cache of active, open connections that are reused by incoming queries. Libraries like `pg` (PostgreSQL) or `mongoose` (MongoDB) handle this automatically; you configure a maximum pool size, and the driver manages distributing queries across available connections.

nodejs.title: Explain how memory management and Garbage Collection work in the V8 engine.
nodejs.description: Describe the generational strategy V8 uses to free up heap memory.
nodejs.answer: The V8 engine divides heap memory into two main spaces: the Young Generation (New Space) and the Old Generation (Old Space). The Young Generation is small and optimized for short-lived allocations, employing a fast 'Scavenge' algorithm to collect memory. Objects that survive multiple garbage collection cycles are promoted to the Old Generation, which is managed using slower but more comprehensive 'Mark-Sweep' and 'Mark-Compact' algorithms.

nodejs.title: What is the difference between spawn() and exec() in Node.js?
nodejs.description: Candidates are frequently asked to compare these two child_process methods to evaluate their understanding of stream-based vs buffer-based processes.
nodejs.answer: The main difference is that `spawn` runs a process and streams its output in real-time, making it ideal for large volumes of data or long-running processes. Conversely, `exec` runs a command in a shell and buffers the entire stdout/stderr output before returning it in a callback, which can fail if the buffer size exceeds its limit (default 1024KB). Use `spawn` for streaming data and `exec` for running simple commands with small execution outputs.

nodejs.title: How do you implement rate limiting in a Node.js Express application?
nodejs.description: Interviewers want to see how you protect APIs from abuse or brute-force attacks at the application level.
nodejs.answer: Rate limiting is typically implemented using middleware like `express-rate-limit` which tracks incoming IP requests. For distributed systems, a fast, memory-based store like Redis is used to maintain request counts across multiple application instances. When a client exceeds their limit, the middleware intercepts the request and returns a `429 Too Many Requests` HTTP status code.

nodejs.title: Explain the concept of REPL in Node.js
nodejs.description: A fundamental question about the Node.js interactive environment often asked in early screening rounds.
nodejs.answer: REPL stands for Read, Eval, Print, and Loop. It represents a simple, interactive computer programming environment that takes single user inputs, evaluates them, and returns the result to the user. You can start the Node.js REPL simply by typing `node` in your terminal, making it highly useful for testing quick JavaScript snippets and debugging code.

nodejs.title: What is the purpose of the assert module in Node.js?
nodejs.description: This assesses the candidate's understanding of native testing and assertion mechanisms within Node.js.
nodejs.answer: The native `assert` module provides a set of assertion functions for verifying invariants and writing basic unit tests without external frameworks. If an assertion fails, it throws an `AssertionError` which halts execution or is caught by a test runner. For example, `assert.strictEqual(1, 1)` verifies value and type equality, ensuring code behaves as expected.

nodejs.title: How does the Node.js 'dns' module differ from browser-based DNS resolution?
nodejs.description: Tests deep understanding of Node's networking capabilities and how it resolves domain names.
nodejs.answer: The `dns` module in Node.js provides actual network DNS resolution capabilities, whereas browsers rely entirely on the operating system's underlying resolution. It offers two categories of functions: those that use the underlying OS facilities (like `dns.lookup`), and those that perform actual DNS queries over the network without thread-pool blocks (like `dns.resolve`). This allows Node.js servers to perform advanced DNS operations like MX or TXT record lookups dynamically.

nodejs.title: What are 'Event Emitting' patterns and how does error-first callback convention relate to them?
nodejs.description: Asks the candidate to compare two fundamental asynchronous patterns in Node.js.
nodejs.answer: Event Emitting patterns decouple the producer from consumers using an event channel where multiple listeners can react to an event independently. In contrast, the error-first callback convention is a 1-to-1 asynchronous pattern where a single callback function handles the immediate result or failure of an operation, with the error object passed as the first argument. While callbacks are great for single async results, EventEmitters are better suited for handling streams of multiple events over time.

nodejs.title: How do you handle circular dependencies in Node.js?
nodejs.description: Tests structural coding knowledge and understanding of how CommonJS loads modules.
nodejs.answer: In CommonJS, when a circular dependency occurs, a module might receive an unfinished, partially filled copy of the exported object from the module it depends on to prevent infinite loops. To resolve this, you can restructure your code to extract the shared dependency into a third module, or defer access to the exported module's properties by requiring them inside functions instead of at the top-level of the file.

nodejs.title: What is the use of the 'os' module in Node.js?
nodejs.description: Tests familiarity with Node's built-in modules for interacting with the host system.
nodejs.answer: The `os` module provides operating system-related utility methods and properties. It allows developers to query system architecture, available memory, CPU cores, and network interfaces. This module is vital when optimizing applications, such as determining how many child workers to spawn based on the available CPU cores using `os.cpus().length`.

nodejs.title: Explain how to read and write large files using streams in Node.js
nodejs.description: Asks for a practical coding/architectural explanation of stream usage for file operations.
nodejs.answer: To read and write large files efficiently without exhausting system memory, you should use `fs.createReadStream()` and `fs.createWriteStream()`. This processes the file in small, sequential chunks (typically 64KB) instead of loading the entire file into RAM. You can connect them using the `.pipe()` method, which automatically manages the flow speed (backpressure) between the reader and writer.

nodejs.title: What is the difference between string concatenation and path.join/path.resolve?
nodejs.description: Tests knowledge of cross-platform compatibility and absolute vs relative path resolution.
nodejs.answer: Manual string concatenation can cause errors across different operating systems because Windows uses backslashes (`\`) while POSIX systems use forward slashes (`/`). `path.join()` joins path segments using the platform-specific separator. `path.resolve()` goes a step further by always returning an absolute path, resolving relative segments against the current working directory (`process.cwd()`).

nodejs.title: What is the purpose of the 'zlib' module in Node.js?
nodejs.description: Tests knowledge of native compression tools to reduce network payloads.
nodejs.answer: The `zlib` module provides compression and decompression functionalities using Gzip, Deflate, and Brotli algorithms. It is commonly used in HTTP servers to compress outgoing response bodies on the fly, reducing network transfer sizes and improving load times. Because it is stream-based, it can compress and decompress large files with minimal memory overhead.

nodejs.title: How does Node.js handle multi-threading behind the scenes if JavaScript is single-threaded?
nodejs.description: Clarifies the distinction between JS execution and the underlying platform architecture.
nodejs.answer: While the JavaScript execution engine (V8) runs on a single thread, Node.js offloads heavy asynchronous operations to C++ APIs and libuv. Libuv maintains a thread pool (default size of 4) to execute blocking tasks like cryptography, compression, and file system I/O in parallel. Once a background thread finishes a task, it notifies the main thread to execute its callback via the event loop queue.

nodejs.title: What is the purpose of the HTTP Agent in Node.js?
nodejs.description: Evaluates knowledge of connection persistence and HTTP pooling on the client-side.
nodejs.answer: The `http.Agent` class manages connection persistence and reuse for HTTP clients in Node.js. By default, it pools TCP connections to speed up multiple consecutive requests to the same host by reusing existing sockets (Keep-Alive). This avoids the overhead of establishing a new TCP handshake for every outgoing request, significantly boosting client performance.

nodejs.title: What are the main security risks of using the 'eval()' function in Node.js?
nodejs.description: Tests security awareness regarding dynamic code execution.
nodejs.answer: Using `eval()` poses severe security risks because it executes arbitrary strings as JavaScript code with the privileges of the running process. If user input is passed into `eval()`, an attacker can perform Remote Code Execution (RCE), access environment variables, manipulate the file system, or shut down the server. To run dynamic code safely, use the built-in `vm` (Virtual Machine) module to execute scripts in a sandboxed context.

nodejs.title: How do you monitor a Node.js application's memory usage in production?
nodejs.description: Tests production readiness, DevOps skills, and knowledge of monitoring tools.
nodejs.answer: You can monitor memory usage programmatically using `process.memoryUsage()`, which outputs heap total, heap used, external memory, and RSS (Resident Set Size). For production monitoring, APM tools like New Relic, Datadog, or PM2 are preferred to track these metrics over time. Additionally, taking heap snapshots using the `--inspect` flag or the `heapdump` package helps diagnose memory leaks using Chrome DevTools.

nodejs.title: Explain how the 'crypto' module secures sensitive data in Node.js
nodejs.description: An introduction to cryptographic principles and hashing within Node.
nodejs.answer: The `crypto` module provides cryptographic functionality, including wrappers for OpenSSL's hash, HMAC, cipher, decipher, sign, and verify methods. For sensitive data like passwords, you should use slow hashing algorithms like `pbkdf2` or external libraries like `bcrypt`. For data transit encryption, the module allows you to create secure symmetric (AES) and asymmetric (RSA) encryption schemes.

nodejs.title: What is the 'vm' (Virtual Machine) module and when would you use it?
nodejs.description: Tests advanced knowledge of sandboxed code execution in Node.
nodejs.answer: The `vm` module enables compiling and running JavaScript code within V8 Virtual Machine contexts. This is useful when you need to execute untrusted scripts or plugins in a separate, isolated sandbox environment to prevent them from accessing global state or the filesystem. However, it is important to note that the `vm` module is not a fully secure sandbox against malicious code, and additional isolation like processes or containers is recommended for extreme security.

nodejs.title: How do you implement microservices communication in Node.js?
nodejs.description: Evaluates architectural patterns for building distributed Node.js systems.
nodejs.answer: Node.js microservices typically communicate via synchronous HTTP/gRPC APIs, or asynchronously using message brokers like RabbitMQ, Apache Kafka, or Redis Pub/Sub. gRPC is preferred for high-performance, low-latency communication because it uses Protocol Buffers over HTTP/2. For decoupled systems, message-driven communication using events ensures high reliability and availability even if some services are temporarily down.

nodejs.title: What are 'unhandledRejections' and how do they differ from 'uncaughtExceptions'?
nodejs.description: Tests error propagation knowledge in asynchronous vs synchronous execution flow.
nodejs.answer: An `uncaughtException` occurs when an error is thrown in synchronous code but is never caught by a `try-catch` block, immediately crashing the process if not handled. An `unhandledRejection` occurs when a Promise is rejected, but no `.catch()` block or try-catch handles it. In modern Node.js, unhandled promise rejections will also terminate the process with a non-zero exit code to prevent state corruption.

nodejs.title: How do you run Node.js processes as a background service?
nodejs.description: Tests operational knowledge on keeping Node.js apps alive permanently.
nodejs.answer: To run Node.js in the background and ensure it automatically restarts on crashes or system reboots, process managers like `PM2` are standard. Alternatively, you can configure your application as a system service using `systemd` on Linux hosts. These tools handle logging, clustering, and resource monitoring, abstracting process management away from manual terminal execution.

javascript.title: Explain 'this' keyword in JavaScript.
javascript.description: 'this' is a common source of confusion. Candidates should be able to explain its context-dependent nature.
javascript.answer: The `this` keyword in JavaScript refers to the context in which the function is executed. Its value is determined by how a function is called, not where it's declared. In a method, `this` refers to the object itself; in a simple function call, it refers to the global object (window in browsers, undefined in strict mode). Arrow functions lexically bind `this`, meaning they inherit it from their parent scope.

javascript.title: What are closures in JavaScript?
javascript.description: Closures are a core concept demonstrating deeper understanding of scope and function execution.
javascript.answer: A closure is the combination of a function bundled together (enclosed) with references to its surrounding state (the lexical environment). In simpler terms, a closure gives you access to an outer function's scope from an inner function. Even after the outer function has finished executing, the inner function can still access variables from its parent scope. This allows for data encapsulation and maintaining state.

javascript.title: Explain event delegation.
javascript.description: Candidates should understand how to optimize event handling, especially in large DOM structures.
javascript.answer: Event delegation is a technique where you attach a single event listener to a parent element, instead of attaching individual listeners to multiple child elements. When an event fires on a child element, it bubbles up to the parent, where the single listener catches it. This improves performance and memory usage, especially for dynamic lists, as you don't need to re-attach listeners for new elements.

javascript.title: What is hoisting in JavaScript?
javascript.description: Understanding hoisting is key to predicting behavior of variable and function declarations.
javascript.answer: Hoisting is a JavaScript mechanism where variable and function declarations are moved to the top of their containing scope during the compilation phase, before code execution. This means you can use a variable or call a function before it has been declared in the code. However, only declarations are hoisted, not initializations. `let` and `const` declarations are hoisted but are in a 'temporal dead zone' until initialized.

javascript.title: How do you handle asynchronous operations in JavaScript?
javascript.description: Assesses knowledge of modern async patterns beyond just callbacks.
javascript.answer: JavaScript handles asynchronous operations primarily using Promises, Async/Await, and to a lesser extent, callbacks. Callbacks are functions passed as arguments to be executed later. Promises provide a cleaner way to handle asynchronous results, representing a value that may be available now, or in the future. Async/Await is syntactic sugar built on Promises, making asynchronous code look and behave more like synchronous code, improving readability and error handling.

javascript.title: What is the event loop?
javascript.description: A deeper dive into JavaScript's concurrency model, crucial for understanding non-blocking I/O.
javascript.answer: The event loop is a core part of JavaScript's concurrency model, which allows JavaScript to perform non-blocking I/O operations despite being single-threaded. It continuously checks if the call stack is empty. If it is, it takes the first message from the message queue (task queue) and pushes it onto the call stack for execution. This mechanism ensures that long-running operations don't block the main thread.

javascript.title: Explain prototypal inheritance.
javascript.description: A fundamental concept for understanding how objects inherit properties and methods in JavaScript.
javascript.answer: Prototypal inheritance is the mechanism by which JavaScript objects inherit properties and methods from other objects. Every JavaScript object has an internal `[[Prototype]]` property, which points to another object (its prototype). When you try to access a property on an object, if it's not found on the object itself, JavaScript looks up the prototype chain until it finds the property or reaches the end of the chain (null).

javascript.title: What are `let`, `const`, and `var`? What are their differences?
javascript.description: A very common and essential question about modern JavaScript variable declarations.
javascript.answer: `var` declarations are function-scoped and are hoisted to the top of their function or global scope. They can be re-declared and re-assigned. `let` declarations are block-scoped, meaning they are limited to the block (e.g., if statement, loop) in which they are declared. They are hoisted but not initialized, leading to a 'temporal dead zone', and cannot be re-declared but can be re-assigned. `const` declarations are also block-scoped, hoisted into a temporal dead zone, cannot be re-declared, and cannot be re-assigned after initial assignment. They are used for values that should not change.

javascript.title: What is the purpose of the `bind`, `call`, and `apply` methods?
javascript.description: Tests understanding of explicit function context manipulation.
javascript.answer: The `call`, `apply`, and `bind` methods are used to explicitly set the `this` context for a function. `call()` invokes the function immediately with a specified `this` value and arguments passed individually. `apply()` also invokes the function immediately with a specified `this` value, but arguments are passed as an array. `bind()` returns a *new* function with `this` permanently bound to a specified value, without executing it immediately.

javascript.title: What is JSON and why is it commonly used?
javascript.description: A basic but essential question about data interchange formats.
javascript.answer: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight data-interchange format. It is human-readable and easy for machines to parse and generate. JSON is built on two structures: a collection of name/value pairs (like a JavaScript object) and an ordered list of values (like a JavaScript array). It's widely used for transmitting data between a server and web application, serving as an open standard data format.

javascript.title: How does `try...catch` work in JavaScript?
javascript.description: Essential for robust error handling in synchronous and some asynchronous code.
javascript.answer: The `try...catch` statement is used to handle exceptions (errors) in JavaScript. Code that might throw an error is placed inside the `try` block. If an error occurs within the `try` block, the execution of the `try` block is stopped, and control is transferred to the `catch` block. The `catch` block receives the error object as an argument, allowing you to gracefully handle the error and prevent the program from crashing. An optional `finally` block will execute regardless of whether an error occurred.

javascript.title: What is debouncing and throttling?
javascript.description: Important optimization techniques for improving performance on frequently triggered events.
javascript.answer: Debouncing and throttling are techniques used to limit the rate at which a function is called, primarily for performance optimization. Debouncing ensures that a function is only executed after a certain period of inactivity (e.g., resize events, search input). Throttling limits the execution of a function to at most once in a given time frame (e.g., scroll events, button clicks). Both help prevent performance issues caused by excessive function calls.

javascript.title: What is the difference between client-side and server-side rendering?
javascript.description: Addresses architectural understanding in modern web development.
javascript.answer: Client-side rendering (CSR) means the browser receives a minimal HTML file, then JavaScript takes over to fetch data and build the DOM in the user's browser. Server-side rendering (SSR) means the server processes the request, fetches data, renders the full HTML page, and sends it to the browser. CSR offers better interactivity and faster subsequent page loads, while SSR provides faster initial load times and better SEO, as content is available immediately.

javascript.title: Describe the concept of 'scope' in JavaScript.
javascript.description: Fundamental for understanding variable accessibility and function execution.
javascript.answer: Scope in JavaScript determines the accessibility of variables, functions, and objects in some particular part of your code. It dictates where these items can be accessed and used. JavaScript primarily has three types of scope: Global scope (accessible anywhere), Function scope (variables declared inside a function are only accessible within that function), and Block scope (variables declared with `let` or `const` inside a block, like an `if` statement or `for` loop, are only accessible within that block).

javascript.title: What are modules in JavaScript?
javascript.description: Assesses knowledge of modern JavaScript's approach to code organization and reusability.
javascript.answer: JavaScript modules are a way to organize code into separate, reusable files. They allow developers to break down large programs into smaller, manageable pieces, enhancing maintainability, readability, and preventing global namespace pollution. Modules use `export` to expose specific variables, functions, or classes, and `import` to use them in other files. This system enables better dependency management and code sharing.

javascript.title: How do you make an AJAX request?
javascript.description: Fundamental for understanding how web applications communicate with servers.
javascript.answer: AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML) allows web pages to update asynchronously by exchanging data with a web server behind the scenes. Traditionally, `XMLHttpRequest` (XHR) objects were used to make AJAX requests. Modern JavaScript often uses the `fetch` API, which is a promise-based mechanism to make network requests. `fetch` provides a more powerful and flexible feature set for making web requests and handling responses, typically returning a Promise that resolves to the `Response` object.

javascript.title: Explain how prototypal inheritance differs from classical inheritance.
javascript.description: Tests a deeper understanding of JavaScript's object model compared to other languages.
javascript.answer: Classical inheritance, common in languages like Java or C++, is class-based, where objects are instances of classes, and classes inherit from other classes. Prototypal inheritance, native to JavaScript, is object-based. Instead of classes, objects inherit directly from other objects (their prototypes). There are no 'classes' in the traditional sense; an object can serve as a prototype for another object, allowing it to inherit its properties and methods. ES6 classes are syntactic sugar over JavaScript's existing prototypal inheritance.
